Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Goal Create a DuckDB SQL query Source Table event_log Columns case_id activity timestamp Logic Count events per case_id Exclude cases where the count is greater than 6 Return all events rows for the remaining cases count less than or equal to 6

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: To filter based on an aggregate calculation count per group I need to use a GROUP BY clause combined with a HAVING clause or a Window Function or a Subquery or CTE

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Approach 1 Window Functions Count over partition by case_id adds a column to every row indicating the size of the case Then filter where this column is less than or equal to 6 Approach 2 IN Subquery Select case_ids where count is less than or equal to 6 then select all from event_log where case_id IN that subquery Approach 3 Join Aggregate table to get case counts filter join back to original table Approach 4 HAVING Standard

Name: Validation - PE
Text: SELECT all FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id HAVING count star less than or equal to 6 is invalid because SELECT all with GROUP BY usually requires all non aggregated columns to be in the group key which defeats the purpose of returning individual events

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: This is usually the most efficient and concise way in modern SQL dialects like DuckDB

Name: Abductive Reasoning - IND
Text: DuckDB syntax usually handles this via a subquery or CTE if you want to filter on a window function result directly without an outer query wrapper or using the standard pattern

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: WITH case_counts AS SELECT case_id FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id HAVING COUNT star less than or equal to 6 SELECT e all FROM event_log e JOIN case_counts c ON e case_id equals c case_id

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: SELECT all FROM event_log WHERE case_id IN SELECT case_id FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id HAVING COUNT star less than or equal to 6

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: The IN clause or SEMI JOIN approach is often cleaner conceptually for filtering groups The Window function approach is cleaner if you want to keep the count visible The IN clause is arguably easier to explain for a benchmark prompt

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: This is robust standard SQL and answers the prompt perfectly

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Table event_log Yes Columns case_id Yes Logic Count greater than 6 exclude Keep less than or equal to 6 Yes Result Full events Yes SELECT all

Name: Backtracking - IND
Text: Actually checking the docs simulated DuckDB supports QUALIFY Query SELECT all FROM event_log QUALIFY COUNT star OVER PARTITION BY case_id less than or equal to 6 This is extremely concise

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: I will provide the Standard SQL approach Subquery as it guarantees compatibility and understanding I will stick to the subquery IN clause It is the textbook solution for Filter table by aggregate condition
